coredata.dc:title: Alkali-assisted facile and scalable preparation of fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon dots with excellent optical and biological properties from thioctic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yiheng
Dong, Linxu
Bao, Jiangkai
Hu, Yang
Song, Ru
Shi, Zhuqun
Yang, Quanling
Xiong, Chuanxi
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of high-performance heteroatom-doped fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from renewable biomass or biometabolites hold an extensive attention. Here, we report an excess sodium hydroxide (NaOH) assisted method for the preparation of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) at lower temperatures by using thioctic acid (TA). Excessive NaOH can promote the thermal decomposition process of thioctic acid in the hydrothermal process, thereby realizing rapid reaction rate (critical time 1 h) and scalable synthesis at low temperature (120 °C). The amount of NaOH and the reaction time were identified as the main factors affecting the synthesis, and the reaction of NaOH with disulfide bonds contributed the most to the generation of fluorescent S-CDs. This in-situ sulfur doping process resulted in excellent fluorescence properties, high relative quantum yield of 11.5%, low biotoxicity, good biocompatibility and excellent solubility, etc. In addition, this as-prepared fluorescent S-CDs showed excellent concentration dependence on ferric ions (Fe3+) and can achieve high-precision and selective detection. This work may provide a high-efficiency synthesis strategy for S-CDs for biological and detection applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Thioctic acid
Sulfur doping
Carbon dots
Alkali-assisted

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-doped CDs) under different NaOH concentrations
Contrast experiment: hydrothermal reaction of octanoic acid with sodium hydroxide of different concentrations
NaOH-accelerated hydrothermal preparation of fluorescent S-CDs
Structural and optical characteristics of the as-prepared S-CDs
Applications of S-CDs: cell imaging and ion detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of the mixture with different raw material ratios under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp (a), and their emission spectra at excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm (b–g).

The FT-IR spectrum (a–b), UV–Vis adsorbtion spectrum (c–d), TEM images (e–g) and relative quantum yield values (h) of the mixture with different raw material ratios; the schematic illustration of the synthetic route from octanoic acid and thioctic acid (h); the UV–Vis spectrum (i) and the emission spectra (j) at excitation of 340 nm of the hydrothermal product from octanoic acid.

The emission spectra of the mixture with different reaction time from 0 h to 10 h at excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm (a–h); size distribution (i–j), TEM image (k) and relative quantum yield values (l) of the mixture with different reaction time.
HRTEM image and lattice fringe (a), XPS survey (b) and XPS S2p (c), C1s (d), O1s (e) spectra and the fluorescence properties (f) of the purified 1:40–10 h S-CDs product.

Cell viability of L929 cells after incubation with 1:40–10 h S-CDs at different concentrations (a); Fluorescence micrograph of L929 under bright field and excitation at 364 nm (b); The emission spectrum (c) and relative intensity I/I0 values (d) of 1:40–10 h S-CDs aqueous solutions mixed diverse metal ions at 100 μmol/L; The emission spectra of 1:40–10 h S-CDs aqueous solutions upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions excited by 364 nm (e) and the dependence of the relative intensity I/I0 values on the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–70 μmol/L.

head.ce:title: Alkali-assisted facile and scalable preparation of fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon dots with excellent optical and biological properties from thioctic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Life Sciences, Wuhan University of Technology, Luoshi Road 122, Wuhan, 430070, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Life Sciences, Wuhan University of Technology, Luoshi Road 122, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Luoshi Road 122, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Luoshi Road 122, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of high-performance heteroatom-doped fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from renewable biomass or biometabolites hold an extensive attention. Here, we report an excess sodium hydroxide (NaOH) assisted method for the preparation of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) at lower temperatures by using thioctic acid (TA). Excessive NaOH can promote the thermal decomposition process of thioctic acid in the hydrothermal process, thereby realizing rapid reaction rate (critical time 1 h) and scalable synthesis at low temperature (120 °C). The amount of NaOH and the reaction time were identified as the main factors affecting the synthesis, and the reaction of NaOH with disulfide bonds contributed the most to the generation of fluorescent S-CDs. This in-situ sulfur doping process resulted in excellent fluorescence properties, high relative quantum yield of 11.5%, low biotoxicity, good biocompatibility and excellent solubility, etc. In addition, this as-prepared fluorescent S-CDs showed excellent concentration dependence on ferric ions (Fe3+) and can achieve high-precision and selective detection. This work may provide a high-efficiency synthesis strategy for S-CDs for biological and detection applications.

Graphical Abstract: Alkali-assisted facile and scalable preparation of fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon dots from thioctic acid.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are a class of 0-dimensional advanced carbon materials with photoluminescence (PL) properties, and their structures are generally spherical or quasi-spherical morphology of several nanometers. Since it was first reported by Sun et al., in 2006 [1], the synthesis and application of CDs have developed incredibly rapidly [2–4]. CDs have been explored for a variety of excellent and unique properties, such as good fluorescence properties, well dispersion, high stability, low biotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, and have attracted intensive and continuous attention from researchers [5]. Based on these fascinating features, CDs hold a wide range of potential applications combining Light-emitting devices [6], catalysis [7], sensor detection [8], biological imaging [9] and other fields. During this stage, various synthesis methods of carbon dots, including hydrothermal method, oxidation method, electrochemical synthesis, ultrasonic and microwave-assisted synthesis [10], have also been successively developed. Among them, the hydrothermal synthesis method utilizing organic matter has received the most attention due to its simple operation, facile and rapid reaction, and excellent PL performance. At the same time, in order to realize the environmental protection of the synthesis process and control the cost, many renewable and cheap biomass [11,12] have been confirmed to be hydrothermally prepared for fluorescent carbon dots. In the hydrothermal environment of high temperature and high pressure, biological organic matter will undergo decomposition, carbonization, nucleation and other processes and eventually form fluorescent CDs of several nanometers [10]. Therefore, the factors that affect the speed of these processes (reaction time, temperature, reactant concentration, pressure etc.) have become the main influencing factors in the regulation of CDs synthesis. However, at a higher temperature (>200 °C), the precursor aqueous solution will reach more than 1.54 MPa which lead to some disadvantages of dangerous, higher equipment structural strength requirements and high energy consumption. How to achieve facile and efficient synthesis of CDs at lower temperatures is also one of the challenges faced by researchers.

Compared with the traditional commonly used metal-based quantum dots (CdTe quantum dots [13], metal sulfide quantum dots [14], CsPbBr3 QDs [15], etc.), the fluorescence of metal-free quantum dots including CDs, Si QDs [16] and sulfur QDs [17] processed super excellent biocompatibility and biotoxicity. This unique advantage makes fluorescent CDs show broad application prospects in biological fields (such as antibacterial, cell imaging). The low fluorescence performance (quantum yield QY<10%) limits its further application expansion. In order to improve the fluorescence properties, the promotion strategy of heteroatom (N-doping, S-doping, etc.) doping fluorescent CDs was developed [18–22]. The doping of heteroatoms can effectively enhance the fluorescence properties of carbon dots to obtain higher QY by changing the surface properties and intrinsic electronic [23]. These heteroatom-doped CDs were used in a variety of applications such as detecting copper ions [24,25], mercury ions [26], and tartrazine [27]. Zhou et al. [10] used ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source to increase the QY of undoped carbon dots from 5.31% to 28.46% by a one-step hydrothermal method at 180 °C. However, the commonly used nitrogen sources are small molecular amines or amides [10,28] (such as ethylenediamine, DMF, etc.) that are not friendly to organisms and the environment, and they all have certain toxicity and are not conducive to production. The strategy of using sulfur doping can also improve the fluorescence performance of CDs and use a more biocompatible sulfur source.

Sulfur is one of the important elements in the metabolism of nature and organisms [29]. Sulfur is mainly present in the metabolic process of organisms in the form of proteins and enzymes. Therefore, these bio-based sulfur-containing organics are ideal raw materials for the preparation of high optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, sustainability, and low cost, of which thioctic acid (TA) is one of the most common and readily available. TA is an octacarbon fatty acid containing disulfide bonds. The C6 and C8 positions of the five-membered ring in the molecule are connected by a disulfide bond. Its structure and function are highly similar to vitamins B, so it can be regarded as a vitamin analog. On the base of these unique properties, TA has been widely concerned for its excellent potential for application in the biological field [30–33]. Similarly, thanks to its internal disulfide bonds and other organic structures, TA can be used as a carbon source and a sulfur source to directly react to prepare sulfur-doped carbon materials without other raw materials, such as sulfur-doped porous carbon materials for supercapacitor materials [34]. To the best of our knowledge, there are almost no reports on the hydrothermal preparation of sulfur-doped carbon dots from TA. The only work reported by Ding et al. [35], they tried to prepare nitrogen-sulfur co-doped quantum dots (N, S-CDs) by reacting TA with ethylenediamine at 250 °C for about 20 h. The process conditions are harsh, high reaction temperature (>250 °C), high-cost, long synthetic cycle and the employment of toxic ethylenediamine nitrogen source. Therefore, in order to solve the above shortcomings and fill the process gap, the work on the synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with TA as a raw material under different process conditions is still worthy of in-depth research and exploration.

In this work, we report a novel method for the preparation of sulfur doped carbon dots (S-CDs) at lower temperatures using excess sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and TA. Excessive NaOH can promote the thermal decomposition process of thioctic acid in the hydrothermal process, thereby realizing rapid (critical time 1 h) and large-scale preparation at low temperature (120 °C). Through comparative experiments and orthogonal experiments, we explored the principle that NaOH promotes the formation of S-CDs in the entire reaction process, and at the same time explored the relationship between reaction time and raw material ratio, so as to obtain the best synthesis condition and provides a novel efficient route for the preparation of S-CDs. After purification, the morphology, structure, composition, and fluorescence properties of S-CDs were characterized and confirmed. In addition, the prepared product exhibits excellent biocompatibility, ultra-low biotoxicity, selectivity for ferric ions, and highly sensitive detection performance, making it promising in the fields of live cell imaging, fluorescent dyes and fluorescent probes application prospects.

Analytical reagent used: thioctic acid (TA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), quinine sulfate and soluble metal salt (KCl, ZnSO4, Na2SO4, CaCl2·2H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, CuSO4, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3, Ba(NO3)2, MgCl2) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai). Milli-Q water was used throughout all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-doped CDs) under different NaOH concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.04 g (1 mmol), 0.08 g (2 mmol), 0.2 g (5 mmol), 0.4 g (10 mmol), 0.8 g (20 mmol) and 1.6 g (40 mmol) NaOH were added into seven beakers respectively. 20 mL water and 0.206 g (1 mmol) thioctic acid were then added to each beaker and stirred until fully dissolved to form a mixed precursor solution. Subsequently, 20 mL of the precursor solution was added to a 100 mL hydrothermal kettle and placed at 120 °C for reaction for 3 h to prepare fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs). The products were further subjected to dialysis treatment using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 kDa to remove unreacted and other small molecule residual impurities. The final pure fluorescent sulfur-doped carbon dots were obtained from the retention solution inside the dialysis bag and stored at low temperature (5 °C) in the dark. According to the molar ratio of thioctic acid to sodium hydroxide these samples were named 1:1–3 h, 1:2–3 h, 1:5-3 h, 1:10-3 h, 1:20-3 h, 1:40-3 h, respectively (Table S1). The contrast experiment of replacing thioctic acid with octanoic acid was carried out under the same ratio condition (Table S2).

For the hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent S-CDs at different times: first, several groups: 1:40 precursor reaction solution containing 0.206 g (1 mmol) TA and 1.6 g (40 mmol) NaOH was prepared and then loaded into a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated to 120 °C for 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h and 10 h, respectively. After the reaction, a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 kDa was also used for dialysis treatment to remove unreacted and other small molecule residual impurities. Finally, pure fluorescent S-CDs were obtained and stored at low temperature (5 °C) in the dark condition. These samples were named 1:40-1 h, 1:40-2 h, 1:40-4 h, 1:40-6 h, 1:40-8 h, 1:40–10 h, respectively (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contrast experiment: hydrothermal reaction of octanoic acid with sodium hydroxide of different concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: 0.04 g (1 mmol), 0.08 g (2 mmol), 0.2 g (5 mmol), 0.4 g (10 mmol), 0.8 g (20 mmol) and 1.6 g (40 mmol) NaOH were added into seven beakers respectively. 20 mL water and 0.144 g (1 mmol) octanoic acid were then added to each beaker and stirred until fully dissolved to form a mixed precursor solution. Subsequently, 20 mL of the precursor solution was added to a 100 mL hydrothermal kettle and placed at 120 °C for reaction for 3 h to prepare fluorescent products. After the reaction, the reaction solution was obtained for direct ultraviolet irradiation and fluorescence detection (Table S4).

100 μL of L929 (mouse fibroblast cell) culture medium was added to each well of a 96-well plate and then cultured in an incubator for 24 h, the cell density is 50,000 cells/mL. Subsequently, 10 μL of different concentrations (0, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 500 μg/mL) of 1:40–10 h S-CDs samples was added to each well and cultured for another 24 h 5 μL of freshly prepared Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was added to the each well and incubated for 1 h for efficient cell staining. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured with a microplate reader. Finally, cell viability was determined by the absorbance ratio of the L929 cells incubated with S-CDs to that of blank group. All experimental groups were provided with 3 parallel samples. For the cell imaging, the cells of the experimental group (500 μg/mL) were placed under an inverted fluorescence microscope and excited at 375 nm UV. The cell images were then observed.

First, all metal salts and water were prepared in a solution based on a specific proportion in Table S5, and the concentration was set to 1 mmol/L. All detection experiments for metal ions were performed by the same procedure. For a typical procedure, 0.3 mL (1 mmol/L) metal ion solution was added into 2.7 mL 1:40–10 h S-CDs aqueous solution and then fully vibrated for about 5 min until completely mixed. The emission spectra of the mixture were recorded under the excitation of 364 nm.

The fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded using the potassium bromide tablet method using a PerkinElmer Spectrum Two FITR spectrometer (USA). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was measured by a UV-1810 spectrophotometer (Yoke, China) with a collection wavelength of 190–1100 nm. The microscopic morphology and lattice fringes of the samples were measured by a JEOL-2100 transmission electron microscope (Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence properties of samples: excitation spectrum, emission spectrum, relative quantum yield, etc., were tested by a RF-5310 P (Shimadzu, Japan) fluorescence spectrometer. The cell morphology and fluorescence imaging photos were obtained by a Zeiss LSM 510 fluorescence microscope (Germany). Photographs of all samples under sunlight and UV light (365 nm 20 W) were taken by Canon 800D digital camera (Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NaOH-accelerated hydrothermal preparation of fluorescent S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Thioctic acid (TA) is a non-water-soluble substance and cannot be prepared into TA aqueous solution. Therefore, it needs to react with equimolar sodium hydroxide (NaOH) to generate a light-yellow water-soluble sodium thioctate (TA-Na) solution for further hydrothermal reaction. According to existing reports, pure sodium thioctate aqueous solution and ethylenediamine need to react at 250 °C for 20 h to prepared N S co-doped CDs. However, as shown in Fig. 1 a, the obtained product by pure TA-COONa (1:1–3 h sample) after being hydrothermally heated at 120 °C for 3 h was lighter in color, and no fluorescence appeared under ultraviolet light irradiation. The fluorescence spectrum analysis can also prove that the product had no fluorescence properties, which indicated that pure sodium lipoate was very stable and difficult to synthesize fluorescent product at 120 °C. With the constantly increase of the concentration of NaOH, i.e., the increase of the concentration of residual NaOH in the reaction precursor solution, the product showed a significant fluorescence phenomenon under same conditions (Show in Fig. 1). The fluorescent products were identified as sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) in the subsequent discussion. It indicated that more NaOH can effectively promote the thermal decomposition of TA. As shown in Fig. 1a, when the concentration ratio of TA: NaOH was further increased from 1:1 to 1:40, the color of the product also gradually became darker from light color, which revealed that higher NaOH concentration would produce more fluorescent S-CDs. Under the UV irradiation of 365 nm, the visible and measurable fluorescence of the product was also significantly enhanced. As shown in Fig. 1b–g and Fig. S1, the intensity of the fluorescence spectrum of the product was also continuously enhanced. The above results all indicated that NaOH can effectively improve the efficiency of preparing S-CDs from TA and reduce the reaction temperature.

In order to further shed light on the mechanism of the NaOH accelerated hydrothermal preparation of S-CDs, we directly detected and analyzed the obtained samples with different NaOH concentrations by FTIR and UV–Vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a–b, strong peaks at 2950 cm−1 and 1710 cm−1 corresponded to C–H groups and CO groups in the TA molecule. And peaks at 3450 cm−1, 2660 cm−1 and 1060 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of O–H, C–S, C–O groups, respectively. The organic characteristic infrared peaks of the obtained reactants were significantly reduced with the increase of NaOH concentration. The peaks at 2950 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching and rocking vibrations of organic framework C–H were weakened. The characteristic peak of CO bond at 1710 cm−1 in TA firstly shifted to 1615 cm−1 after reaction with NaOH to form -COONa groups. It then showed a significant weakening and a shift to higher wavenumbers revealing a reduction in -COONa groups. In the reaction of hydrothermal preparation of fluorescent carbon dots, organic matter mainly undergoes reaction processes such as decomposition, carbonization and nucleation. These infrared results clearly indicated that the increase of NaOH concentration would significantly increase the decomposition rate of TA under hydrothermal conditions. Similarly, the UV–Vis spectrum (shown in Fig. 2c–d and Fig. S2) can also illustrate this promotion process. For the 1:1–3 h sample without fluorescence, no characteristic peak at 274 nm was observed which corresponded to the CC π-π* transition of the CDs. And this characteristic peak appeared and increased with the increase of raw material ratio. In the visible light range, especially at 400–500 nm (Fig. 2d), the absorption of the product also increased significantly, indicating that the color of the product was gradually deepened, which was consistent with the photograph observed in Fig. 1a.

In order to further explore the effect of alkali on the hydrothermal decomposition process of TA, TEM was used to observe the microstructure changes of the prepared products under different alkali concentrations. As shown in Fig. 2e, the 1:1–3 h sample showed a typical organic matter morphology. Combined with the previous FT-IR analysis, it can be known that it was undissolved TA, and did not have fluorescence performance. However, for the 1:5-3 h sample, it can be clearly observed that the organic matter began to form nano-dot morphology (Fig. 2f), and was wrapped by most of the unreacted organic matter, which made the 1:5-3 h sample show low fluorescence performance (QY = 1.9%). At higher alkali concentrations, the 1:40-3 h sample showed typical structure characteristics of quantum dots, and no significant unreacted TA was observed. The size of 1:40-3 h S-CDs was mainly distributed in the range of 0.5–3.5 nm, with an average particle size of 2.18 nm (Fig. 2g). The relative quantum yield of the sample reached 6.1% (Fig. 2h). These results indicated that the increase of sodium hydroxide concentration can effectively promote the decomposition of TA to form S-CDs. At the same time, their QY values of samples with different alkali concentrations were shown in Fig. 2h. It can be clearly seen that the QY values increased with the increase of NaOH.

According to the basic reaction principle, the main reaction sites of excess NaOH and TA were disulfide bonds and carboxyl groups. The hot alkaline environment can break the disulfide bond and also promote the decarboxylation reaction, both of them could lead to the decomposition of TA in the hydrothermal process. In order to further confirm which reaction contributed more to the accelerated synthesis of fluorescent S-CDs, we selected octanoic acid as a comparison substance instead of TA and different concentrations (1:1 to 1:40) of NaOH to conduct comparative experiments under the same conditions of 120 °C for 3 h. Shown in Fig. 2h, octanoic acid and TA had close molecular structures, the difference was that octanoic acid only contained carboxyl groups without disulfide bonds so that excess NaOH mainly reacted with the carboxyl groups of octanoic acid. As shown in Fig. 2i–j and Fig. S3, all the obtained products were colorless and transparent solutions after hydrothermal treatment, and there was no obvious photoluminescence phenomenon under UV irradiation. For the UV–Vis spectrum of octanoic acid hydrothermal product, there was no absorption in the visible region (i.e., colorless and transparent) and no characteristic peaks of CC transition appeared. Similarly, at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, the product samples of all components did not show fluorescence emission properties. These results showed that the hydrothermal octanoic acid did not form any fluorescent CDs under this condition, and also suggested that the reaction between NaOH and carboxyl groups was not the key factor for the formation of CDs. This also indirectly showed that the reaction between NaOH and disulfide bond was the key point of NaOH-accelerated process of TA to prepare fluorescent S-CDs.

To further explore the optimal process conditions for the hydrothermal preparation of S-CDs from NaOH-accelerated process, we selected the raw material ratio (TA:NaOH) of 1:40 and reacted at 120 °C as the invariant condition for different times to explore the effect of time. And for chemical reactions, longer reaction times mean higher conversion rates. Fig. 3 a–h showed the fluorescence spectrum of the samples under different reaction times, and there was no fluorescence signal of the raw material before the reaction (1:40-0 h sample). The product began to show a weak fluorescence response after 1 h of reaction, which meant that a small amount of fluorescent substance began to be formed, and after 2 h of reaction, obvious fluorescent phenomenon appeared and it emitted a clear blue light under UV light irradiation. As the reaction time increased to 8 h, the fluorescence intensity of the product increased significantly, and when it was further increased to 10 h, the increase rate of the fluorescence intensity became small, indicating that the reaction was almost complete after 10 h. Correspondingly, with the prolongation of the reaction time, the color of the product continued to deepen (Fig. S4). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum also showed a significant improvement in visible light region Fig. S5. Fig. 3i–j showed the particle size distribution at 1:40-3 h and 1:40–10 h samples. It can be seen that the average particle size of S-CDs obtained with the extension of reaction time increased from 2.18 nm to 3.78 nm (Fig. 3k), which may be caused by the further growth of S-CDs due to the decomposition of more TA under a long reaction time. Similarly, their QY values (Fig. 3l) also showed an increasing trend under longer reaction time. Which also indicated that the increase of reaction time led to the increase of total TA conversion rate.

The increase of NaOH concentration and the prolongation of time were beneficial to the complete decomposition of TA and the formation of fluorescent S-CDs. Effects of the reaction time and the concentration of NaOH on the fluorescence intensity of the product can form a simple orthogonal relationship. On the basis of different times and different concentrations of NaOH as a comparative orthogonal experiment, the peak intensity values of the product under excitation at 340 nm was measured and shown in Fig. S6. It can be clearly observed that both the reaction time and the concentration of NaOH expressed a positive effect on the product. The intensity of the 1:20-4 h S-CDs group was very close to that of the 1:40-2 h group, which indicated that the prolonging reaction time and the increase of NaOH concentration can be equivalently replaced. For the raw material ratio of 1:40 component group, there was a large amount of unreacted NaOH raw materials in the product after the reaction was completed, which were not easy to separate and post-process, and increase the cost. According to the results discussed above, it can be known that the same S-CDs can be obtained by prolonging the reaction time to reduce the amount of sodium hydroxide used, which was beneficial to reduce the cost and the difficulty of post-processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characteristics of the as-prepared S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a showed the high-resolution TEM images of the 1:40–10 h S-CDs product. It can be observed at low magnification that the product presented a relatively uniform dot-like morphology of several nanometers with significant quantum dot morphology characteristics. This fluorescent product of NaOH-promoted hydrothermal from TA were determined to be quantum dot rather than other fluorescent small molecules and macromolecules. Their particle size distribution was relatively uniform, and 50 points were randomly selected to count the particle size as shown in Fig. 3j. Their size was mainly concentrated in 3.0–4.5 nm, and the average particle size was 3.72 nm after Gaussian fitting. The lattice fringes of the product can be clearly observed at higher resolution (Fig. 4a), which indicated well crystallinity. After background noise reduction and measurement, it can be calculated that the measured lattice fringe spacing was 0.204 nm, which agreed with the (100) crystal plane of the fluorescent CDs [28]. XPS test was used to further explore the composition of the prepared product. As shown in Fig. 4b–e, the purified 1:40–10 h S-CDs product contained C, O, S three elements. C element was the main constituent element of CDs and occupied the main content. Oxygen was mainly derived from the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs, while S element also occupied a certain content. After peak fitting processing (Fig. 4c), it can be seen that the sulfur valences were [−1] and [−2], corresponding to the S–S and C–S bonds, respectively [17,36]. In addition, the XPS spectra of C1s and O1s were shown in Fig. 4d–e. Peaks at 284.3, 285.3 and 384.3 corresponded to C–C, C–O and CO bonds, respectively [37], indicating the characteristics of CDs in the product and the presence of oxygen-containing ligands on the surface. Peaks at 533.0 and 531.9 corresponded to the C–O and CO bonds of oxygen element respectively. All these results indicated the existence of oxygen-containing organic ligands on the surface of S-CDs products.

This shows that sulfur was doped into the fluorescent CDs product and effectively improved the fluorescence performance. The UV–Vis and fluorescence properties of the purified S-CDs were shown in Fig. 4f. In the UV–Vis spectrum, an absorption peak at 247 nm was observed which corresponded to the π−π* transition of CC bonds in S-CDs [10]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak near 340 nm corresponded to the n−π* transition of CO bonds, which indicates that the product contains an organic oxygen-containing (carboxyl groups) ligand [10]. The emission wavelength still showed dependence on excitation wavelength, the maximum emission wavelength was determined to be 467 nm, and the corresponding optimal excitation wavelength was 364 nm. Under the excitation of 364 nm wavelength, the emission spectra of S-CDs and quinine sulfate were measured under the same ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence test conditions, respectively. By the relative quantum yield (QY) test compared to that of quinine sulfate [17], it can be calculated that the relative QY of the prepared S-CDs was 12.7%, which exceeded most of the undoped carbon dots, suggesting excellent fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of S-CDs: cell imaging and ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that compared with other nitrogen-doped CDs with higher QY, there was no additional use of highly toxic heteroatom-doped sources (such as ethylenediamine) in this work. At the same time, benefit to the bio-renewable raw materials and heavy metal-free structure, the S-CDs and their surface ligands possessed better biocompatibility and lower biotoxicity, which showed unique insights in biological applications. Fig. 5 a showed the toxicity evaluation test, the L929 cells were co-cultured with different concentrations of 1:40–10 h S-CDs for 24 h. At small concentrations (0–0.1 mg/mL), the 1:40–10 h S-CDs did not show any toxicity and even benefited cell growth when the blank control group was used as the standard [38]. When the concentration reaches 0.5 mg/mL, the cell viability at 24 h is still 92.3%, which is significantly higher than other reported heavy metal-free quantum dots, revealing its excellent cytocompatibility. The photographs of the L929 cells after 24 h of culture at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL were shown in Fig. 5b. Compared with the control group without 1:40–10 h S-CDs, no significant changes were found in the shape, outline and number of cells, and no rupture and other aberrations appeared. This once again indicated that excellent biocompatibility of 1:40–10 h S-CDs. At the same time, the 1:40–10 h S-CDs could enter the cell and emit green fluorescence under UV light irradiation for imaging, and the internal structure and shape of the cell can be clearly displayed, which made the 1:40–10 h S-CDs can be used as an excellent fluorescent dye for living cell imaging.

As another unique application of QDs, the feasibility of using 1:40–10 h S-CDs as fluorescent probes for ion detection in aqueous solutions was explored. The emission spectra and intensities of the1:40-10 h S-CDs at the optimal excitation wavelength of 364 nm were recorded after mixing with different kinds of metal ion solutions. As shown in Fig. 5c, the wavelength of the emission spectrum of 1:40–10 h S-CDs mixed with different metal ions did not change, while the intensity of the emission peak decreased to different degrees depending on different ions in a same concentration. The integrated area of the emission peak intensity of the comparison group without metal ions was taken as the standard I0, and the emission peak intensities of other detection groups were taken as I. The relative results (I/I0) shown in Fig. 5d can be obtained after the integration and comparison calculation. It can be observed that Fe3+ exhibited the strongest quenching effect on 1:40–10 h S-CDs at the same concentration, which fluorescence intensity was significantly lower than those of other ionic groups, while the quenching effect of most other ions on the product was very slight (I/I0> 95%, such as Na+, K+, Mg2+), which enabled 1:40–10 h S-CDs to be used as efficient and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. To explore the detection range and precision for Fe3+, the emission intensities under different concentrations of Fe3+ was tested and shown in Fig. 5e. Its emission intensity decreased sequentially with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, and there was no significant red shift and blue shift in emission wavelength. After integral and comparison calculation, the relative intensity values at different concentrations were shown in Fig. 5f. A clear significant linear relationship between the concentration of Fe3+ ions and the relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 was observed in the range of 0–70 μmol/L. The standard curve of detection can be obtained by linear fitting, and its correlation coefficient R2 reached 0.9862, suggesting excellent linear relationship and detection accuracy. However, when the concentration of Fe3+ ions was in the range of 80–100 μmol/L, it significantly deviated from the linear relationship with the I/I0. Therefore, the as-prepared 1:40–10 h S-CDs were confirmed to have a selective and high-accuracy detection ability for Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–70 μmol/L.

In this work, we report for the first time that biorenewable and biocompatible thioctic acid was selected as carbon and sulfur sources, and excess NaOH was used to assist in the successful preparation of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) with excellent performance under a facile temperature condition of 120 °C. According to the research results in this paper, excess NaOH was found to exhibit a significant promoting effect on the reaction, which can promote the decomposition of TA in the hydrothermal reaction and enhanced the synthetic rate of S-CDs. The comparative experiments further showed that the main contribution originated from the cleavage of disulfide bonds by NaOH. Both the concentration of NaOH and the reaction time have been confirmed to be positive effects on the production of S-CDs, and the concentration of NaOH was the main factor, which can provide a new strategy for the efficient preparation of S-CDs. After purification treatment, the prepared 1:40–10 h S-CDs showed uniform particle size distribution, mainly concentrated in 3–4.5 nm with an average particle size of 3.72 nm and high crystallinity. XPS analysis confirmed that the product was successfully doped with sulfur and its relative quantum yield reaches 12.7%. The prepared 1:40–10 h S-CDs exhibited very low biotoxicity (cell viability greater than 92.3% at 0.5 mg/mL concentration) and can be used directly as a fluorescent dye in the field of live cell imaging. In addition, the 1:40–10 h S-CDs were explored to be used as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of ferric ions in the concentration range of 0–70 μmol/L. In a word, this synthesis process can efficiently prepare 1:40–10 h S-CDs and realize in-situ sulfur doping at lower reaction temperature and energy consumption. During this whole process, no toxic chemicals were used which was beneficial to subsequent biological applications and environmental protection. The excellent fluorescence and biological properties enable this prepared S-CDs to be excellent fluorescent materials and probes in optical and biological fields.

Yiheng Song: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Linxu Dong: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data Curation, Methodology, Jiangkai Bao: Formal analysis, Data Curation, Methodology, Yang Hu: Investigation, Data Curation, Methodology, Ru Song: Data Curation, Zhuqun Shi: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Quanling Yang: Writing – review & editing, Chuanxi Xiong: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. ZR202103020328).
